did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and active learning strategies for skin lesion classification. Specifically, it employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including U-net and Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN) for segmentation, and evaluates classifiers such as Random Forest. The study applies active learning strategies (e.g., Margin Sampling, Entropy) to select informative samples for training the classifier to diagnose skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving an extensive evaluation of active learning strategies for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (U-net, FCN, Random Forest), datasets, and results (e.g., 93% accuracy with Margin Sampling), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Lesion through Active Learning Strategies
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin lesions, specifically non-melanoma and melanoma, by exploring active learning strategies to reduce the need for large annotated datasets. The authors evaluated segmentation using U-net and Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN), feature extraction via Border-Interior Classification (BIC), and classification using Random Forest across three datasets. Various active learning criteria, including Margin Sampling, Entropy, and Least Confidence, were tested to select informative samples for training. The experimental results demonstrated that the combination of FCN segmentation, BIC features, and Random Forest yielded the best performance. Notably, the Margin Sampling strategy achieved approximately 93% accuracy using only 35% of the training data compared to traditional full-set learning. The findings conclude that active learning significantly reduces annotation costs while maintaining high diagnostic accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as a malignant tumor with accelerated growth and greater lethality; one of the two main categories of skin cancer studied.
Identified as the most frequent type of skin cancer in Brazil; grouped with melanoma as the primary diagnostic targets.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (non-melanoma vs. melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on the classification of skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories (non-melanoma and melanoma). It evaluates classifiers such as Random Forest and uses active learning strategies to select samples for training the classifier. Performance is measured by classification accuracy (e.g., 93% accuracy).
The study evaluates segmentation strategies using U-net CNN and Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN) to extract features (Border-Interior Classification) for the subsequent classification task.

datasets_used.name: Three datasets
datasets_used.description: The study utilized three datasets for experimental evaluation, though specific names (e.g., ISIC, PH2, etc.) are not explicitly stated in the provided text chunks. The datasets contained skin lesion images for classification tasks.

use.training: Used for training classifiers with active learning strategies (e.g., Margin Sampling used 35% of the training set).
use.validation: Used for validation scenarios as part of the extensive experimental evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of segmentation, feature extraction, and classification models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracies (e.g., about 93% with Margin Sampling) and compared performance qualitatively against traditional learning approaches without statistical significance testing.

three_datasets.notes: The paper reports that the combination of FCN segmentation with manual correction, Border-Interior Classification (BIC) extractor, and Random Forest classifier showed the best performance. The Margin Sampling strategy achieved approximately 93% accuracy using only 35% of the training set, compared to the traditional learning approach which required the entire set. Specific quantitative results for each of the three individual datasets are not provided in the text.

architecture_used.main_model: U-net CNN, Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN), Random Forest (RF)

training_details.best_performing_configuration: FCN segmentation with manual correction + BIC extractor + Random Forest classifier
training_details.margin_sampling_performance: Approximately 93% accuracy using 35% of the training set

preprocessing_techniques: Manual expert review/correction of segmentation masks

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions that data augmentation was performed as part of the experimental setup, but does not specify the particular techniques (e.g., geometric transforms, color transforms) or their settings in the provided text.

conclusions: The combination of Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN) segmentation with manual expert correction, the Border-Interior Classification (BIC) feature extractor, and the Random Forest classifier yielded the best performance for skin lesion classification.

The Margin Sampling active learning strategy achieved high classification accuracy (approximately 93%) using only 35% of the training data, outperforming traditional learning approaches that require the entire dataset.

Active learning selection strategies enable achieving high accuracies faster (with fewer learning iterations) and with a significantly smaller amount of labeled samples compared to traditional learning approaches.
Active learning contributes significantly to the diagnosis of skin lesions by beneficially reducing the annotation costs for medical specialists.
important_remarks: The study evaluates only two lesion classes (non-melanoma and melanoma), limiting its generalizability to other dermatological conditions or multi-class scenarios.
The paper does not explicitly name the three datasets used, which hinders reproducibility and assessment of potential dataset bias (e.g., demographic or geographic skew).
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported; performance comparisons are based solely on classification accuracy metrics.
The best-performing segmentation strategy involved 'manual expert correction' of FCN outputs, introducing potential human-in-the-loop bias and limiting full automation.
The claim of 'about 93% accuracy' is an aggregate result across three datasets; specific per-dataset performance metrics are not provided in the text.
The paper does not report computational costs or inference times, despite acknowledging computational cost as a motivation for using active learning.
No external validation on independent, unseen datasets is described; the evaluation relies on internal validation scenarios within the three provided datasets.
Data augmentation is mentioned but specific techniques and hyperparameters are not detailed in the provided context, limiting reproducibility.
